reasoning:
The Fed has ceased shrinking its overall balance sheet but explicitly continues to allow agency MBS to run off, reinvesting the proceeds into Treasury bills. Thus, the MBS portfolio strictly decreases or remains flat, with very rare occurrences of trivial positive adjustments (e.g., millions on a $2 trillion portfolio due to delayed settlements). 

Looking at the most recent data trends, mid-month balances almost always remain flat (as from Dec 31 to Jan 14), with large redemptions (drops of $10 billion to $20 billion) clustering around the end of the month. 

- For Jan 25, 2026: This captures data up to Jan 21. Modest mid-month settlement bumps are rare; it will most likely be flat (no increase) or show a small decline. 
- For Feb 17, 2026: This captures data through mid-February. The balances will have undergone the reliable, large end-of-month redemption drop in late January, significantly lowering the value below the Jan 18 baseline. It is near-certain to be lower.

Per the system instruction to ensure that our uncertainty increases with the forecast horizon and our probabilities pull closer to 0.5 over longer durations, the Feb 17 probability is given slightly progressively closer to 0.5 than the short-term Jan 25 date, although both are overwhelmingly likely to resolve NO.
